paper_title: Pollen likely seasonal factor in inhibiting flu-like epidemics. A Dutch study into the inverse relation between pollen counts, hay fever and flu-like incidence 2016–2019

methods.description: Process of collecting and preparing public data sets from the Dutch Central Bureau of Statistics (CBS) and RIVM for analysis of pollen counts, hay fever medication revenues, and flu-like incident reports.

steps.description: We used the public data sets of the Dutch Central Bureau of Statistics (CBS) about the weekly pollen count in The Netherlands and revenues of (anti-histamine) medication against the effects of hay fever (2016 = 100), and the data from RIVM (the Dutch State Institute for Public Health) about weekly flu-like incident reports at the primary medical care per 100,000 Dutch citizens.
steps.searchable_summary: fetch public data sets from CBS and RIVM for pollen counts and flu-like incidents

steps.description: The overlapping data period of both data sets is taken, which runs from week 1 of 2016 till week 10 of 2019 (n = 166).
steps.searchable_summary: filter data sets to overlapping period from 2016 to 2019

steps.description: Regarding the incidence of flu-like symptoms, we also calculated the weekly change compared to the previous period to get an indication of the flu-like epidemic life cycle progression.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate weekly change in flu-like symptoms

methods.description: Statistical procedures used to test the null hypotheses regarding the correlation between pollen counts, hay fever symptoms, and flu-like reports.

steps.description: We formulated three statistical null hypotheses H1(0). There is no inverse correlation between pollen count and flu-like reports. H2 (0). There is no inverse correlation between pollen count and weekly changes in flu incidents. H3 (0). There is no inverse correlation between hay fever symptoms and flu-like reports.
steps.searchable_summary: formulate null hypotheses for correlation analysis

steps.description: When inspecting the data sets regarding pollen counts and flu-like consults at primary medical care in The Netherlands, it is clear that there are a few pollen bursts, especially during Spring, and then at a lower level during Summer.
steps.searchable_summary: inspect data for seasonal patterns in pollen counts and flu-like consults

steps.description: Given the burst like phenomenon, it is not strange that we found no clear correlation between pollen count and the incidence of flu-like medical consults for our data set (correlation coefficient: .07).
steps.searchable_summary: calculate correlation coefficient between pollen count and flu-like consults
